Anthropic just dropped the upgraded Claude 3.5 Sonnet, and while they’ve kept the same name, it’s packed with improvements. The most exciting part? Claude can now control computer screens, which is a huge step toward fully agentic workflows. While this feature is still in beta, it allows developers to automate tasks like navigating software and testing code directly on-screen. It’s part of Anthropic’s push to focus on practical developer tools, setting them apart from more general AI models like OpenAI’s offerings.  What’s interesting about Anthropic’s strategy is how much they’re leaning into the API business. They’re growing their API usage five times faster than OpenAI, and this release is clearly aimed at developers. By improving Claude’s coding abilities and adding experimental features like computer use, they’re focusing on niche capabilities that make a real difference in developer environments. While OpenAI is making headlines with its versatility, Anthropic is making waves by doubling down on what they do best.  Another exciting feature coming soon is Claude 3.5 Haiku. It promises even faster performance, with a focus on efficiency for those who need high-speed, high-performance AI for their coding tasks. This could make Anthropic an even more attractive option for developers and companies looking for tailored AI solutions.  The big takeaway here is how Anthropic is positioning itself as a major player in the API and developer tool space. They’re differentiating themselves with a specialized focus on growth in areas that matter most for devs. I’m looking forward to testing out the new tools and seeing how these updates improve workflows across the board. Yall disrepecting Anthropic like Claude hasn’t held us down since day 1 #anthropic #claude #sonnet   Claude is an AI assistant developed by Anthropic that’s designed to understand and generate natural language, but it’s also surprisingly capable when it comes to helping with code.  In a coding context, Claude can: 	•	Read and explain code — You can paste in a snippet or entire file, and it will walk you through what it does in plain English. 	•	Debug issues — If you’re getting an error or unexpected behavior, Claude can help pinpoint the problem and suggest fixes. 	•	Refactor and optimize — It can rewrite code to make it cleaner, faster, or easier to maintain. 	•	Generate new code — You can describe the functionality you want, and Claude can produce boilerplate code, full functions, or even complete scripts. 	•	Assist with documentation — It can create docstrings, inline comments, and README files for better code clarity. 	•	Guide learning — Claude can break down programming concepts and provide examples tailored to your skill level.  Because it’s trained to follow instructions carefully, Claude is especially good at iterating on your code ideas — you can give feedback (“make this more efficient,” “switch this from Python to JavaScript,” “add error handling”), and it will adapt the code accordingly.

Anthropic just dropped a 33-page guide on how to build Claude skills. Sounds normal. Except buried inside that guide are two example skills structured around a trading bot. One that trades prediction markets. At a 68.4% win rate. People are reporting $300 to $1,500 a day from it. The guide also walks through setting up stock trading, portfolio management, and scoring 2,800 individual stocks. All inside what's supposed to be a general skills tutorial. Burying a trading bot inside a developer doc is the most Anthropic thing I've ever seen. A 68% win rate means nothing without a Sharpe ratio. That's fair. Win rate alone doesn't tell you about risk-adjusted returns. But Anthropic built a framework that lets you create autonomous agents with memory, tools, and decision-making. And the example they chose to teach it with was a money-making machine. They could've used any example. They picked trading. That tells you where this is going. Sonnet 4.6 just replaced 90% of what I use Opus for and it costs 40% less 👇  I ran the exact same prompt through three Claude models back to back. No cherry-picking.  Same task every time: build a complete SaaS pricing page with blur effects, popups, and interactive elements.  Here's what each model actually produced:  Sonnet 4.5 → Basic pricing page. Gets the job done but nothing special. Missing details, minimal styling, bare-bones layout.  Opus 4.6 → Massive upgrade. More UI elements, extra polish, blur effects actually working, popup components included. This is what you'd expect from the flagship model.  Sonnet 4.6 → At first glance it looks identical to Opus. Same quality, same polish. But scroll down. It proactively added a full comparison table that Opus didn't even think of. Extra sections breaking down features so users can actually tell the difference between tiers. Details that make the page usable, not just pretty.  The breakdown: Sonnet 4.5 → basic output, cheapest Opus 4.6 → premium output, most expensive Sonnet 4.6 → near-premium output with extras, 40% cheaper than Opus  So unless you're doing complex multi-step reasoning or research-level tasks, Sonnet 4.6 handles everything else. Coding, UI builds, content generation, data analysis — it's all there.  I've already switched my daily driver. The cost savings add up fast when you're running hundreds of prompts a day.  Save this so you know which model to use for what.  Which Claude model are you using daily right now? Drop it in the comments 👇  Follow for more real AI build comparisons — no hype, just output. Claude Monet (1840–1926) was a French painter and a leading figure of Impressionism, a movement that revolutionized art in the late 19th century. He was born in Paris but spent much of his childhood in Le Havre, where he developed an early interest in drawing and the natural landscape.  Monet began his artistic career by creating caricatures, but his path changed when he met artist Eugène Boudin, who introduced him to painting outdoors (en plein air). This approach—working directly in nature—became central to Monet’s style. Rather than focusing on precise details, he sought to capture the effects of light, atmosphere, and movement.  In the 1860s and 1870s, Monet worked alongside artists such as Pierre-Auguste Renoir and Edgar Degas. Together, they challenged traditional academic painting, which emphasized historical or mythological subjects and polished technique. Instead, the Impressionists painted modern life—landscapes, city scenes, and everyday moments—using visible brushstrokes and vibrant colors.  Monet’s painting Impression, Sunrise (1872) played a crucial role in the movement. When it was exhibited in 1874, a critic mockingly used the term “Impressionism” to describe the work’s loose style. The artists embraced the name, and it became the title of their new artistic movement.  Throughout his life, Monet was deeply interested in how light changes over time. He often painted the same subject repeatedly at different times of day and in different weather conditions. This can be seen in his series of haystacks, the Rouen Cathedral series, and views of the Houses of Parliament. These works show how color and perception shift depending on light and atmosphere.  In 1883, Monet moved to Giverny, a small village in northern France, where he would live for the rest of his life. There, he created a carefully designed garden with a pond, water lilies, and a Japanese-style bridge. This garden became the inspiration for his most famous works, the Water Lilies. These large-scale paintings focus on reflections, color, and surface, often eliminating the horizon line and creating an almost abstract effect.  Despite facing financial struggles early in his career, Monet eventually achieved recognition and success. In his later years, he continued to paint even as his eyesight deteriorated due to cataracts. This condition influenced the colors and forms in his later works, making them even more expressive.  Claude Monet died in 1926 at the age of 86. Today, he is regarded as one of the most important artists in history. His work not only defined Impressionism but also paved the way for modern art, influencing generations of artists who followed.
